coredata.dc:title: Microfluidic synthesis of optically responsive materials for nano- and biophotonics
coredata.prism:publicationName: Advances in Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Koryakina, Irina G.
Afonicheva, Polina K.
Arabuli, Konstantin V.
Evstrapov, Anatoly A.
Timin, Alexander S.
Zyuzin, Mikhail V.
coredata.dc:description: Recently, nanomaterials demonstrating optical response under illumination, the so-called optically responsive nanoparticles (NPs), have found their broad application as optical switchers, gas adsorbents, data storage devices, and optical and biological sensors. Unique optical properties of such nanomaterials are strongly related to their chemical composition, geometrical parameters and morphology. Microfluidic approaches for NPs’ synthesis allow overcoming the known critical stages in conventional synthesis of NPs due to a high rate of heat/mass transfer and precise regulation of synthesis conditions, which results in reproducible synthesis outcomes with the desired physico-chemical properties. Here, we review the recent advances in microfluidic approach for synthesis of optically responsive nanomaterials (plasmonic, photoluminescent, shape-changeable NPs), highlighting the general background of microfluidics, common considerations in the design of microfluidic chips (MFCs), and theoretical models of the NPs’ formation mechanisms. Comparative analysis of microfluidic synthesis with conventional synthesis methods is provided further, along with the recent applications of optically responsive NPs in nano- and biophotonics.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, nanomaterials demonstrating optical response under illumination, the so-called optically responsive nanoparticles (NPs), have found their broad application as optical switchers, gas adsorbents, data storage devices, and optical and biological sensors. Unique optical properties of such nanomaterials are strongly related to their chemical composition, geometrical parameters and morphology. Microfluidic approaches for NPs’ synthesis allow overcoming the known critical stages in conventional synthesis of NPs due to a high rate of heat/mass transfer and precise regulation of synthesis conditions, which results in reproducible synthesis outcomes with the desired physico-chemical properties. Here, we review the recent advances in microfluidic approach for synthesis of optically responsive nanomaterials (plasmonic, photoluminescent, shape-changeable NPs), highlighting the general background of microfluidics, common considerations in the design of microfluidic chips (MFCs), and theoretical models of the NPs’ formation mechanisms. Comparative analysis of microfluidic synthesis with conventional synthesis methods is provided further, along with the recent applications of optically responsive NPs in nano- and biophotonics.

ce:section.ce:para: Optically responsive nanoparticles (NPs) have emerged as a powerful tool that addresses important issues in biological and optical sensing[1], imaging[2], photovoltaics[3], light-emitting diodes (LEDs)[4,5], photothermal therapy[6], and others. Depending on their nature, NPs possess different optical functionalities. For instance, noble metal or plasmonic NPs can effectively absorb light energy and convert it into heat, which can further induce changes in the surrounding medium[7]. Photoluminescent (PL) NPs is another class of optically responsive materials, which includes quantum dots (QDs)[8], carbon dots (CDs)[9], rare-earth-based NPs[10], and perovskite NPs[11], which can be used as luminescent elements. Apart from PL, nonlinear optical response of NPs, such as near-infrared (NIR)-induced photon upconversion in perovskite nanocrystals, is of great interest for in vivo bioimaging due to the high penetration depth of NIR light into living tissues[12]. Other types of optically sensitive NPs can overcome structural changes themselves in response to external light irradiation. For instance, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are able to change their structural, dynamical and photochemical properties under light irradiation, which can be exploited in photoinduced release of different cargoes loaded in MOFs[13,14] or in all-optical data processing devices[15].

All the above mentioned optical features of NPs strongly depend on their physico-chemical properties, such as size, shape and composition. For example, increasing the size of plasmonic NPs leads to a red shift of the plasmon band. PL of QDs strongly depends on their size and composition[16,17]. It was shown that by adjusting the composition of, e.g., perovskite QDs (the halide ratios in the colloidal nanocrystal solution), PL can be tuned over the whole visible spectra[18,19]. Furthermore, the monodispersity of the resulting NPs is crucial for obtaining a narrow/predictable optically-induced response. It should be stressed that physico-chemical properties of NPs are mostly defined by their synthesis procedure, which usually involves multiple steps when using flask-based chemical synthesis approaches. These methods are not always reproducible due to the poor control over heat and mass transport[20,21].

The mentioned challenges in the synthesis of optically responsive NPs can be solved by means of microfluidics, which provides an outstanding control over synthesis of nanomaterials, resulting in NPs with predictable sizes, shapes, and particle size distributions. Microfluidic technologies enable reproducibility of the synthesis route and scaling up the resulting product. Due to the excellent heat and mass transfer in microchannels of a microfluidic chip (MFC), synthesis time can be reduced[22,23]. Apart from synthesis, MFCs can be endowed with some additional functionalities, such as in situ detection of the obtained NPs[24]. Therefore, it is possible to combine all the synthetic procedures (incl. synthesis, purification, detection) in a single microfluidic platform for the automatization of the NPs synthesis.

In this article, we review the recent progress in the development of controllable synthesis of optically responsive NPs using microfluidics with their further application in nano- or biophotonics (Fig. 1 ). It is important to highlight that such optically responsive NPs can be used as elements in biophotonics, e.g., as multifunctional drug carriers, in optical hyperthermia applications, as catalysts for chemical reactions, as well as in bioimaging. A special focus is placed on the design of an MFC for the synthesis of functional photoactive nanomaterials, and the corresponding physical mechanisms behind it. We provide further additional insights on the mechanisms of NPs’ formation and growth. Finally, we give a comparison of microfluidic and conventional synthesis approaches, and discuss possible bottlenecks/advantages of the microfluidic synthesis alongside with the possibilities of optical performance improval of synthesized NPs for their further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical aspects of microfluidics. Design considerations of a microfluidic system
ce:section.ce:para: Microfluidics regulates the fluids on the micro- and nanometer scale, enabling their controllable mixing, which can be effectively used in the synthesis of functional NPs with defined properties[25–28]. Indeed, microscale implies a higher precision of mixing ratios, reagents’ concentration and diffusion rate[29–31], allowing the adjustment of the reaction time and intensity of mixing. Owing to nano- and micrometer scale, fluid physics differs from macroscopic systems, e.g., surface tension in microchannels of an MFC dominates over gravity force[32]. It should be noted that microfluidics mostly works with laminar flows. Turbulent flow appears only with applied external stimuli such as electric or magnetic fields, acoustic impact, or geometrical obstructions in the microchannels of an MFC[33].

The flows in a microchannel can be described with a classical Navier-Stokes equation of fluid mechanics for incompressible fluids[34]:
(1)

∇ 2

where v is the velocity field, ∇ is the nable operator, p is the pressure, η is the viscosity, ρg is the gravitational force (in terms of the density ρ and the acceleration of gravity g), and ρ
el E is the electrical force (in terms of the charge density ρ
el of the fluid and the external electric field E).

Generally, flows in an MFC are described as (i) continuous, (ii) segmented or (iii) droplet-based, which can be characterized by dimensionless numbers (Table 1 ). One of the most commonly used dimensionless number is the Reynolds number (Re), which represents the ratio between the inertial and viscous forces. Small values of Re indicate laminar flows, in which viscous forces dominate and determine the smooth movement of a liquid. It is assumed that in the microfluidic systems, Re < (1÷10). At high Re, the inertial forces significantly affect the flow and, therefore, turbulence occurs.

In case a liquid flows through a curved channel, its flow is affected by centripetal forces and changes its trajectory. These centripetal forces can be significant, and in order to take them into account, the dimensionless Dean number (De) can be applied, which is the only number that characterizes the flow in a weakly curved channel at increased velocities.

Another dimensionless number is the Capillary number (Ca), which estimates the relative strength of the shear stress compared to the surface tension. In case of droplet microfluidics, the lower the surface tension is, the easier it is to generate fine droplets. At relatively high values of Ca, viscous forces dominate, which leads to the deformation of the droplets by the flow and the appearance of droplets with asymmetric shapes[35].

It is worth mentioning that in the case of droplet-based and segmented flows, the balance of inertial, viscous, and interfacial tension forces determines the droplets/segments formation and restricts their flow. The ratio between the deforming inertial forces and the interfacial surface tension in a fluid can be characterized by the Weber number (We) [36–38]. When We exceeds the critical value of We > 12, the hydrodynamic forces become greater than the stabilizing forces of the surface tension, and the disruption of droplets can occur[38]. As a drawback, We does not consider the liquid viscosity, which affects the droplets formation. In order to take into account the liquid viscosity, the Ohnesorge number (Oh) is introduced, which is defined as the ratio between We and Re [39].

The Bond number (Bo) determines the ratio between the gravitational and surface tension forces. For Bo < 1, the gravitational effects can be neglected. Another dimensionless number is the Péclet number (Pe), which considers diffusivity of a liquid. Pe can be defined as the ratio of convection rate and diffusion rate. In addition, there is the Knudsen number (Kn), which represents the ratio of the mean free path length of the fluid molecules to the characteristic length of an MFC. Kn helps to determine whether it is the statistical mechanics or the continuum mechanics formulation of fluid dynamics that should be used for modelling. Generally, in microfluidic systems, at values of Re < 1 and We/Ca < 1, interphase effects become dominant. All the above mentioned characteristic numbers are often used in theoretical modelling of flows in an MFC.

Based on the flow type (continuous flow, segmented flow or droplet-based flow), the mixing of liquids in an MFC differs. In the case of the continuous flow regime, the mixing occurs only when reagents mutually diffuse on the interphases[40,41]. The main idea of the segmented and the droplet-based flow is to divide a flow into sections of limited volume within the microchannel, i.e. the so-called microreactors. In the case of droplet-based microfluidics, the dispersed phase is surrounded by the continuous phase, while in the case of segmented flow, the dispersed phase interacts with the wall of an MFC. Mixing of the dispersed phase within a drop or a segment can be realized using several techniques (either active or passive)[42].

Furthermore, it is worth mentioning that in micro-dimensional spaces, the liquid (gas) mostly interacts with the surface of the MFC channels, and therefore, with the MFC material. Therefore, the properties of the MFC' material have a significant impact on the flow movement and processes that occur when the fluid interacts with the surfaces of microchannels. For instance, roughness of an MFC' channel can affect the liquid heating up more than a smooth one, since a large surface area is applied for heat transfer. Naturally, the smaller the space for fluid movement, the more significant the influence of the surface properties on the fluid.

The continuous flow regime implies a laminar stream of two or more reagents. In this case, the mixing process is complicated due to the lack of turbulence and small Re. To homogeneously mix liquids in the microchannels, plenty of methods can be applied. Among them active and passive methods can be considered [43,44].

The process of mixing consists of the three phases: when liquids meet (mixing coefficient = 0), diffusion, full mixing. Although the physics of the diffusion is not changing, a timescale/duration of mixing (mentioned steps) is extended or narrowed down depending on the applied mixing method. Diffusion is described by Fick’s laws (10).
(10)
J = − D
∂ C
∂ x
where J is the diffusive flux (mol/(m2s)), D is the diffusion coefficient (m2/s), and C is the concentration of drug in the reservoir (mol/m3).

The mixing of fluids is mainly dependent on diffusion with a very low mixing efficiency. Therefore, it is important to develop different micromixers to increase mixing efficiency in the development of microfluidic systems. Active or passive mixing methods in the continuous flow regime are comprehensively reviewed in Ref.[43].

Passive mixing uses various modifications of the MFC channel’s geometry to enhance mixing without applying external energy (except pressure used to drive the fluid flows). Geometry of an MFC can involve spiral or zigzag channels, embedded barriers and others, which can also be combined with each other. The main advantage of the passive mixing approach is the absence of additional complex parts integrated in an MFC for mixing. However, sometimes the application of a sophisticated topology is not enough, therefore, to ensure an effective mixing of fluids with high viscosity, the introduction of active components is relevant (Fig. 2A).

Active mixing involves external stimuli[44] such as pressure, acoustic field, electric field, magnetic field, electrokinetics, thermal energy, and others (Fig. 2)[43,45], which induce the mutual diffusion of flows. For example, in the theoretical work of Canós Valero et al., mixing was induced by light stimulus and mechanical impact on the fluid. To realize the light-responsive mixing, a vortex was created by the irradiation of a silicon (Si) nanocube with a circularly polarized laser beam. This vortex caused the spiral motion of Au NPs placed around the Si nanocube. This gave rise to convective fluid motion and enhanced fluid mixing[33].

Another type of active flow mixing is pressure-driven mixing that can be realized by introduction of micropumps. Recently, Solano-Arana et al. proposed a pumping Y-shaped micromixer based on peristaltic movements enabling active mixing of fluids, as well as pumping the fluids in an MFC[46] (Fig. 2C).

Acoustically induced microflows are also widely used for mixing of different types of fluids in microfluidic devices. Acoustic-driven micromixers employ bulk acoustic waves, surface acoustic waves and acoustically vibrating microstructures to transfer acoustic energy into liquids and improve their mixing. Among the mixers based on acoustically vibrating microstructures, the ones with an oscillating sharp-edge structure and air bubbles have some benefits, because they can cause strong localized microstreams. Sharp edges are oscillating sidewall microstructures inside the channel that can induce acoustic streaming and further successful mixing. Such an oscillation of sharp edges leads to the volumetric motion of fluid, known as the acoustic micro-flow[47].

Air bubbles can also be used as an active element of an acoustic-driven mixer. For this, the air bubbles are introduced into a channel, and acoustic waves are applied, affecting the bubbles in the fluid. Thus, the bubbles are displaced, causing radial and translational oscillations, which lead to the flow pulsation. Therefore, the bubbles become additional acoustic transmitters and amplify the effect of acoustic waves. The acoustically-excited bubbles can be applied for the development of microvortices and reduction of mixing time to a few seconds. As an example, an acoustic platform based on the combination of oscillatory bubbles and sharp edges in an MFC was shown to effectively mix water and fluorescein within 0.8 ms by Rasouli et al.[48] (Fig. 2B). Acoustic-based mixers can successfully solve the issues of NPs synthesis such as controlling nanoprecipitation. However, acoustic vibrations can lead to the reagents’ heating and, therefore, induce undesirable reactions.

Thermal energy also can be used to increase the mixing efficiency by enhancing the diffusion of two flows with thermal bubbles or thermal effects. In the recent work of Tan et al., the periodic explosive boiling mechanism was used to induce mixing in an Y-shaped microchannel that was modified with a thin layer of microheater (Fig. 2D). Heat-produced bubbles enhanced the mixing of the dye solution from the first channel with the water from the second channel by disturbing the flow in a wiggling manner[49].

Another kind of active mixer is based on magnetic field-driven mixing. Such a mixing can be realized by introducing magnetic NPs or moving structures (a microstirrer or a rotor) into the MFC’s channel and inducing their motion in the fluid by a permanent magnet, an electromagnet or integrated electrodes. This in turn leads to the formation of turbulent flows and the diffusion of liquids.

Hejazian et al.[50] showed a micromixer based on magnetofluidic actuation in an Y-shaped PDMS microchip with two inlets and one outlet. To induce an effective mixing, authors used a ferrofluid consisting of magnetic NPs (10 nm) and neodymium–iron–boron permanent magnets for creating a non-uniform magnetic field. The first inlet channel contained water, whereas the second inlet channel contained ferrofluid. The magnets were incorporated into the slot cut in the middle of the PDMS MFC. The change of distance between the magnets and channel caused the changes of the magnetic field and, therefore, ferrofluid fluctuations in the channel, with further efficient mixing of two fluids.

Despite the obvious benefits (rapid and homogenous mixing) of active mixers, they possess some disadvantages: the whole microfluidic system becomes too intricate with addition of extra parts, which complicates the fabrication process. Beside from that, it should be noted that active mixer affects the kinetics of a reaction as well [51,52].

Droplet-based or segmented flow creates discrete volumes using immiscible phases in order to restrain one phase (dispersed phase) inside the second phase (continuous phase). The resulting drops or segments can be considered as isolated microreactors. It is important to mention that a formed drop does not interact with the channels of an MFC, whereas the segments are restricted by the walls of the channel (Fig. 3A). The droplet/segment formation rate can reach 10 000 droplets/segments per second or more[54], which is favorable for a high output of reaction products.

In microfluidic devices, droplets or segments can be produced using passive or active approaches. Passive droplets/segments formation methods are defined by the geometry of an MFC based on different physical mechanisms acting when the flow of the dispersed phase breaks up into drops in: (i) coaxial flows (co-flowing stream), (ii) elongation of deformed flows in devices with flow focusing (flow focusing), (iii) intersecting flows in a T-shaped device (T-injector)[55] (Fig. 3B).

In the coaxial-flow geometry, generation of drops occurs when a capillary with dispersed phase is placed inside a channel with continuous phase[55,56]. In the case of a flow-focusing MFC geometry, drops or segments are produced by the hydrodynamic focusing (shearing) of the dispersed phase by the continuous phase. T-injector consists of two channels: an inlet channel containing a dispersed phase is perpendicular to the main channel containing a continuous phase. Drops/segments in the T-injector MFC configuration are formed after the dispersed phase enters the main channel, and the continuous phase takes it downstream. In the cases of coaxial and flow-focusing droplet generation, it is crucial to make sure that the dispersed phase does not wet the walls. Surface wetting affects the interfacial tension, which is crucial for the droplet formation.

For each MFC geometry, generation of droplets/segments can occur in three regimes: dripping, jetting, and stable ones. In the case of the dripping regime, drops detach from the dispersed phase in the capillary and are carried downstream by the continuous outer flow. In the jetting regime, dispersed phase forms a jet (thread) and separates to drops when destabilization occurs. Stable co-flow regime does not imply drop formation, since the system is stable.

At the interface of liquid media, the following forces affect the formation of droplets/segments: the viscous force of shear stress, the force of surface tension, and the resistance force to the continuous-phase flow (compression force). The formation of the drop/segment can be regulated by the ratio of the flow rates between two immiscible liquids.

The active methods of droplet/segment formation employ the use of external stimuli. For instance, Yin et al. used an electric field to separate the flow and induce the droplets' formation[57]. This approach adopts a stable multilayer flow as an initial state, utilizing the direct-current (DC), square, and sinusoidal E-fields to trigger the coalescence of the continuous phase and thus “cut” the disperse phase. The size of the resulting droplets can be tuned by adjusting the voltage and the frequency of the electric field. This approach greatly reduces the requirements on the hydrodynamic properties and resolves the wettability issue of the materials for droplet formation and control (Fig. 3.C1).

Another approach that can be used to manipulate the droplets formation/fusion is exposure to the electromagnetic radiation. In the work[59], the fusion and division of the droplets in the MFC channel was demonstrated under laser irradiation. In this case, the mechanism of the droplets’ formation/fusion can be described by the Marangoni effect, in which the surface tension depends on the temperature variation induced by laser heating. This leads to the spatial imbalance of the surface tension and, consequently, to the flow inside and around the drop.

In the work[54] authors demonstrate active droplet formation by using pulsed laser-induced cavitation (Fig. 3.C2). To implement the droplet formation, a topology of two channels with a nozzle-shaped connection between them was used. Laser beam with a wavelength of 532 nm, 15 ns pulse width, and a maximum repetition rate of 100 kHz was focused through an objective lens (100x, NA 0.9) into the pulsed channel filled with water. This caused expanding and cavitation of vapor bubbles that broke the stable oil-water interface and pushed the water into the channel with oil. As a result water-in-oil droplets are formed.
Both these approaches require heat from laser irradiation and can be classified as thermally-activated droplet generators.

Jin et al. demonstrated the microdroplets generation induced by focused surface acoustic waves (FSAW). Surface acoustic waves were concentrated by focused interdigital transducers placed near the channel with water (Fig. 3.C3). When FSAW was applied, the oil-water interface deformed until it broke to a droplet. Authors demonstrated the dependence of the resulting droplets’ diameter on the applied FSAW frequency. For instance, when the FSAW frequency was close to the resonant frequency (35.5 MHz), the droplet diameter reached 100 μm[58].
Thus, active droplet formation approaches demonstrate controllable processes for achieving the generation of stable monodisperse droplets.

It is also worth mentioning that when working with droplet microfluidics, it is essential to ensure the stability of the formed emulsions. To enable stability of droplets, surfactants (abbreviation for surface-active agents) are usually used[60,61]. Surfactants are amphiphilic molecules that reduce the interfacial tension between the reagents of continuous and dispersed phases. In most cases, the choice of an appropriate surfactant is crucial, and we refer to an excellent article describing the smart selection of surfactants[62].

The controlled mixing of fluids discussed above can be successfully applied in the synthesis of functional nano- and micromaterials with precisely defined physico-chemical properties, which can be further used as optically responsive elements. To understand the formation process of nanomaterials, we should consider several models (e.g., LaMer model, Ostwald Ripening and Digestive Ripening, Finke-Watzky mechanism) developed for controlling the morphology and size of NPs during their synthesis[20,63,64]. These models describe the NPs’ formation mechanisms using the conventional wet chemistry methods. LaMer model is considered as the most widespread theory explaining the process of particle formation in the solution. It usually includes four steps, such as (i) supersaturated solution formation, (ii) nucleation, (iii) growth and (iv) agglomeration (Fig. 4 ). At the first step, solute concentration increases up to the maximum (the so-called critical solute concentration). Next, the nucleation process occurs, in which the components of the reaction undergo a “burst-nucleation”, and after that, the concentration of free components in the solution is significantly reduced. The low concentration of components complicates the nucleation process, therefore, the particle growth begins as the components diffuse through the solution[63,64]. Since the growth of the particles can lead to the particle aggregation, it should be carefully controlled to form a stable particle sol. The sol-gel technique is a good example demonstrating the process of particle formation, nucleation and growth[64]. According to the LaMer method, it is vital to separate the nucleation and growth processes in order to form monodisperse particles. However, valuable insights to the metal NPs formation was brought by Polte’s group [65–67]. By applying small angle X-ray (SAXS) it was shown that Au NPs number increases at the beginning of the reaction. This statement disprove “burst” nucleation in Au NPs formation [68,69]. Guardia et al. developed a synthetic protocol for the preparation of iron oxide NPs of various sizes by using decanoic acid as a stabilizer and controlling the heating process[70]. John et al. verified the nanoscale-modified LaMer model for the synthesis of Sn/Pt NPs[71]. The authors used small- and wide-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS/WAXS) to monitor growth of NPs in situ using the LaMer model. It is worth mentioning that the LaMer model cannot explain all the processes during the fabrication of NPs. Therefore, other “non-classical” theories have been developed to explain the mechanism of NPs formation. You and Fang published an excellent review, describing new non-classical theories/models of NPs’ formation[72].

The Ostwald Ripening mechanism of particle growth is based on the change in the solubility of particles depending on their size[73]. Due to the high solubility and high surface energy of small particles in solution, they can be redissolved, and then, further particle growth is achieved[64]. The sol-gel process enabled using silica-based precursors (e.g., tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), tetramethyl orthosilicate (TMOS), etc.) is a representative example explaining the Ostwald Ripening mechanism. First, the silica-based precursors are hydrolyzed. Next, the hydrolyzed silica precursors are polymerized, which results in the formation of a silica matrix. The hydrolysis rate and polymerization are two main factors that define the size of the final particles and their aggregation within the solution. Furthermore, the Ostwald Ripening mechanism was used for the fabrication of hollow NPs[74], for example, hollow TiO2 NPs, where TiF4 solution was applied as a precursor. The hydrolysis of TiF4 in the aqueous solution led to the formation of crystalline aggregates[75]. It was found out that the crystallines located in the centre of aggregates are smaller compared to the crystallines in the outer layers. Therefore, during the synthesis procedure, the inner crystalline aggregates undergo dissolution and accumulate in the outer layers, which leads to the formation of hollow structure.

The Digestive Ripening is a process that is opposite to the Ostwald Ripening. The Digestive Ripening method of nanomaterials’ formation includes three steps: (i) re-dispersing large particles into small particles by the addition of ligands; (ii) extracting the products from the synthetic mixture; (iii) formation of monodisperse NPs by the heating process. The synthesis of Au NPs is a good example that can be described by the Digestive Ripening method[76,77].

The Finke-Watzky mechanism of NPs formation includes two main processes: (i) continuous nucleation, and (ii) autocatalytic growth. This mechanistic model is based on the concept of continuous nucleation followed by the autocatalytic surface growth[78]. The nucleation process is slow, while the autocatalytic growth of particles occurs rapidly. The two-step mechanism of Finke and Watzky is suitable for the synthesis of transition-metal NPs, including Ir, Au, Pt, Rh, Pd, and Ni[79].

Coalescence and Orientated Attachments are similar processes of NPs growth. Once the NPs are attached to each other as building blocks, they agglomerate into a single NP. Here, the reduction of the surface energy of NPs is the driving force, since the total surface area of the united particle is lower than the sum of areas of each individual particle before their coalescence[80]. The Orientated Attachments usually occur in two pathways: (i) NPs can be united by three-dimensional (3D) rotation, which decreases interparticle misalignment, defeating the dislocation of particle-particle barriers; (ii) atom-by-atom reorientation via grain boundary migration. As an example, Yuk et al. demonstrated Coalescence process and Orientated Attachment on Au NPs using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM)[80].

Therefore, microfluidic synthesis can be used to control each step or mechanism in various models of NPs formation. Indeed, this approach was already applied for the large-scale continuous production of hybrid NPs with definite size, shape, structure and properties[20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nanomaterials in MFC for nano- and biophotonics

ce:section.ce:para: Noble metal or plasmonic NPs (e.g., Au, Ag) exhibit localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) owing to resonant oscillations of the electric field of incident light and free electrons in plasmonic NPs, which results in optically-induced heating. The surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band strongly depends on the size, shape and composition of plasmonic NPs. Thereby, the controllable synthesis of plasmonic NPs allows precisely tuning their optical response to the biological transparency region in the NIR optical range[85].

Conventional synthesis of hydrophobic Au NPs was developed by the Brust and Shiffrin[86], where, in order to transfer the resulting Au NPs into water, an additional step of polymer coating should be performed[87]. The whole synthesis procedure takes 7 h[88]. Another conventional approach for the Au NPs synthesis was proposed by Turkevich[89], and then by Frens[90], using wet chemical methods. According to these approaches, citrate-capped Au NPs prepared in aqueous media with the defined size and shape can be synthesized in a controlled manner[91]. The synthesis procedure in this case lasts only 5 min[92].

Microfluidic synthesis can be also applied to enable the controllable synthesis of plasmonic NPs. Abalde-Cela et al. demonstrated a highly controlled synthesis of Au NPs using droplet microfluidics. Use of the droplet microfluidic topology of an MFC enabled simultaneous addition of the reagents (silver salt and ascorbic acid) and further enhanced mixing, as well as addition of surfactant molecules. With surfactant the resulting Au NPs show less amount of longer tips, while surfactant-free synthesis results in a higher number of shorter tips. Applied conditions, such as mixing and surfactant-based synthesis allowed high control over synthesized particle shape (number and spikiness of Au tips), thereby, adjusting optical performance. Duraiswamy et al. combined continuous flow with droplet approach in one microfluidic system for the synthesis of anisotropic Au nanocrystals[93]. This approach allowed to obtain variety of shapes and sizes of Au NPs by tuning the flow rates of reagents, which affected the relative concentrations of Au precursor. In another work, average diameters of spherical Au NPs were tuned with the diameter of the MFC’s inlet. Decreasing the inlet diameter resulted in an increased flow rate of ascorbic acid and final pH of the reaction, and consequently, decrease of the diameter of Au NPs[94]. Recently, Ye et al. employed an S-shaped micromixer for the synthesis of Au nanobipyramids with controllable morphology. The geometry of Au NPs was tuned by changing the flow rate of channels with silver nitrate, ascorbic acid and Au seeds solution[95]. Monodispersed cubic-shaped Au NPs with fast edge-length tuning (53, 58, 70 and 75 nm) were synthesized using a zigzag micromixer (Dean-Flow-Mixer). Sizes and shapes (spheres, cubes or rods) of Au NPs were varied with the concentration by adding halides (NaBr) to the growth solution[96] (Fig. 5A). Uson et al. developed a multistage microfluidic platform for the controlled synthesis of Au nanorods stabilized with poly(ethylene glycol)-methyl ether thiol, which allowed to reduce the reagent consumption by 100 times compared to the conventional batch synthesis. The aspect ratios of the developed nanorods were tuned by adding halide anions (Cl- and Br-) to the synthesis[97]. Microfluidic technique was applied for the self-assembly of Au octahedra into monodisperse 3D supercrystals exhibiting a high SERS signal over extended areas. The intensity of surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) signal increased with increasing size of Au NPs[98] (Fig. 5B). Then, Au NPs micropatterns were synthesized (without adding a reducing agent) and deposited on PDMS surfaces using a microfluidic approach. The location and distribution of Au NPs within PDMS film was controlled by adjusting the ratio of the curing agent and the PDMS base. The obtained patterned freestanding Au-doped surfaces can be applied in optical devices or for biochemical analysis on microchips[99].

As it can be seen, the main tool in microfluidics synthesis is the adjustment of the relative flow rates ratio. Variation of the flow rate affects the relative concentrations of the reagents, which can lead to the changes in resulting particle sizes and shapes. This, in turn, affects the optical parameters of the plasmonic particles. For example, Au nanospheres (50 nm in diameter) show a single resonance band around 520 nm, while Au nanorods demonstrate two resonance bands forming the electronic coherent surface plasmon resonance [100].

In biophotonic application the size and shape of used particles can vary depending on the application. We refer to a comprehensive review on Au NPs synthesis, optical properties and applications in biosensing, biomedical imaging [101].

An attractive class within the family of carbon-based nanomaterials is carbon dots (CDs), which have recently drawn a lot of attention due to their high fluorescence quantum yield, stability against photo-bleaching and low toxicity[102,103]. Optical properties of CDs are related to their structural properties, which are mostly determined by the synthesis method. The synthesis parameters such as temperature, pressure and chemicals can influence the polymerization and carbonization steps during the synthesis of CDs. Basically, one should distinguish doped- and undoped CDs, which demonstrate different optical responses. For more details on the synthesis and optical properties of CDs, we refer to an excellent review[102].

The conventional approaches for CDs’ synthesis include selective solvent heating[104], laser ablation[105], and size control-confined pyrolysis[105]. The entire synthesis duration is estimated to be the range of 2-24 h.

Apart from traditional synthesis approaches, monodisperse CDs can also be synthesized using microfluidics. Indeed, Gomez-de Pedro et al. developed a microfluidic system that consisted of two modules that enabled reagents’ mixing and temperature control of the reaction. The obtained CDs, 3.2 nm in diameter, were further used for the development of an optical pH sensor, since the CDs changed their color in response to the variation of pH values[106]. Recently, uniform CDs with full spectrum fluorescence were synthesized using a microfluidic approach. More accurately, a system of a continuous flow was applied with preheating of the reagents prior to mixing. This way the reaction rate is controlled better, which leads to better heat transfer. Notably, the synthesis procedure was significantly reduced and took only 20 min. Authors claim it was possible due to the rapid heat and mass transfer in microfluidics, which improves mixing and affects the reaction rate. In this work, the ratio of citric acid and urea flow rate was studied. Using microfluidic methods the authors were able to synthesize smaller and more homogeneous carbon dots than by conventional methods. Multiple-color fluorescence emission from CDs was studied in living cells, demonstrating their potential for bioimaging applications[107] (Fig. 6A).

Microfluidic technique can be applied for the synthesis of complex hybrid structures based on CDs and other organic compounds for photonic needs. Indeed, Guo et al. fabricated patterned hybrid films composed of CDs and photonic crystals that demonstrated angular dependence of structural colors, using 3D microfluidics printing technique. Microfluidic scheme to obtain assembly of CDs and photonic crystals represents a two-inlets channel that is connected to the injection nozzle (Fig. 6B). The developed films were further applied for the anti-counterfeiting and coding purposes[108]. The same group reported on the microfluidic synthesis of photonic crystals modified with CDs. The resulting microparticles, composed of poly(methyl methacrylate-butyl acrylate-methacrylic acid) and CDs, demonstrated a highly saturated and angle-independent structural color that solves the problem of optical distortion. Additionally, the hybrid microparticles modified with CDs showed a dual optical response to metal ions, enabling sensing both with fluorescence and structural color[109]. Similar photonic microstructures based on CDs, also synthesized with microfluidic approach, were applied for optical displays and encoding. For this, CDs-based microparticles were incorporated with magnetic Fe3O4(C-Fe3O4) NPs, which ensured their optical and magnetic responsiveness. The microfluidic scheme represented a droplet generator of T-junction type with CDs embedded with polymer as the dispersed phase and methyl silicone oil was exploited as the continuous phase. The fabricated hybrids were utilized as pixels in a display panel that can be switched in the external magnetic field[110].
As CDs demonstrate fluorescence that is shown to be temperature-dependent [111], they have a great potential to be applied in biomedical imaging [112].

Semiconducting NPs or quantum dots (QDs) with dimensions in the nanometer range have been widely studied in the last decades due to their outstanding optical properties, such as narrow emission, tunable wavelength, high photoluminescence (PL), spectral purity, and photochemical stability [113]. Depending on their elemental composition, QDs are classified into four groups: (i) II–VI group (CdS, CdSe, CdTe, ZnO, ZnS, ZnSe, and ZnTe), (ii) III–V group (InN, InP, InAs, InSb, GaN, GaP,GaAs, and GaSb), (iii) IV–VI group (PbS, PbSe, and PbTe) and (iv) IV group (Si)[114,115]. For the detailed information on synthesis and application of QDs, we refer to the excellent review articles[114,116,117]. As for other optically responsive NPs, optical properties of QDs strongly depend on their size, shape and elemental composition. Conventional synthesis of QDs include either top-down (molecular beam epitaxy, ion implantation, e-beam lithography and others) or bottom-up (wet chemistry and vapor phase) approaches[118]. Among the major drawbacks of top-down approach, structural imperfections, and incorporation of impurities in the structure of QDs can be named[119]. Bottom-up synthesis approaches provide higher control over the size/shape of QDs compared to top-down methods; however, they still suffer from poor reproducibility and low rates of precursor mixing, cooling and heating in reaction. Time of conventional synthesis varies for the mentioned methods, for example, by the ion implantation (depending on the parameters) the synthesis time can vary from ms range to a few minutes [120,121], and by the solvothermal method the synthesis time is 8-12 h [122,123].

To overcome the above mentioned limitations, microfluidic technology was applied for the stable synthesis of QDs. The continuous fabrication of QDs (ZnSe/ZnS) in a continuous flow microfluidic system was realized by Kwon et al.[124] The proposed MFC consisted of 3 inlets for precursors, passive mixing zone and heating zone. It was demonstrated that the sizes of ZnSe/ZnS QDs could be easily controlled by changing the flow rates of precursors, which affected the optical properties of QDs. The obtained QDs were further used for the fabrication of a QD-LED device that provided blue emission at 434 nm, excited by a UV LED chip (Fig. 7 ). Wang et al. demonstrated that ZnS-coated CdSe composite QDs can be continuously synthesized in a microfluidic reactor consisting of two syringe pumps that contained CdSe and ZnS precursors. Authors varied the particle sizes and coating thicknesses by adjusting the flow rates[125]. Kikkeri et al. showed that the temperatures used for microfluidic synthesis of QDs can be significantly reduced compared to conventional batch synthesis. For this, a continuous flow system based on two syringe pumps with Cd and Se/Te precursors was developed. The temperature required for continuous flow synthesis was 160 °C (vs. 250-300 °C in the case of batch QDs synthesis approach)[126]. In another work, a continuous flow microfluidic reactor was used to achieve controllable synthesis of ZnSe QDs using a two-stage pathway[127]. First, ZnSe nuclei were formed at low temperature (160 °C), then ZnSe nanocrystals were grown at higher temperature (340 °C). Authors demonstrated the influence of precursor concentration in the reaction medium on the absorbance and PL of the obtained QDs. A universal multistage microfluidic platform based on continuous flow approach was developed for the synthesis of (III–V) QDs. The proposed reactor included up to 6 stages, which were responsible for mixing, aging, sequential growth, shell formation and annealing processes. The versatility of the developed microfluidic systems allowed tuning the temperature and injection profiles to achieve the desired design of QDs. This multi-segmented microfluidic system was also applied to synthesize (PbS, PbSe) QDs (Fig. 7B)[128]. The segment generator was based on a cross configuration of three separate capillaries with precursor solutions (Pb and S) and the carrier fluid. In order to monitor the synthesis process, an in-line fluorescence spectrometer was integrated in the microfluidic system, enabling a rapid screening of a wide range of synthetic conditions. Hu et al. developed a one-step microfluidic approach to fabricate biofunctionalized CdTe QDs for bioimaging purposes. The internalization of these QDs and their imaging properties were further successfully tested in macrophages[129].

Conventional synthesis of QDs, both top-down and bottom-up approaches, have some above-mentioned drawbacks that microfluidics can overcome. Continuous flow can significantly reduce important reaction parameters such as temperature and time of processes, reagent concentration and consumption. Controlling these parameters, microfluidics synthesis allows one to get QDs with the desired emission wavelength and narrow size distribution. Also, one microfluidics device for QDs obtaining can combine several stages of synthesis and an integrated detector for monitoring the synthesis process. Thereby, microfluidics approach shows some benefits compared with the conventional one making synthesis of desired QDs faster and easier to conduct.

Nowadays, perovskite nano- and microparticles are of significant interest due to their outstanding optical and electrical properties enabling their application as lasers, solar cells, photodetectors, and light-emitting diodes (LEDs)[11,130–132]. Nonetheless, cavities of perovskites of various shapes can serve as an active optical medium of microscale photonic devices due to their outstanding lasing properties[133–136]. Conventional synthesis approaches are chemical vapor deposition and hot-injection methods. The synthesis duration can vary from 80 to 120 min.

Zhang et al. reported on a rapid synthesis of CsPbBr3 nanowires (NWs) in a continuous-flow microfluidic device[137]. Perovskite precursors were mixed in a controlled manner, and the time of a reaction was controlled by the flow rate. The obtained perovskite morphologies and their optical performances depended on the flow rates and temperature of a reaction. The authors demonstrate that the developed microfluidic approach promoted the alignment of elongated CsPbBr3 NWs, while conventional batch synthesis resulted in irregular nanorods under the same conditions. In a recent work, a novel microfluidic synthesis approach was proposed for the synthesis of high-quality CsPbBr3 NWs[135]. This method enabled transferring the obtained nanolasers onto any arbitrary surface, which is important for the fabrication of photonic devices with high throughput and excellent control over geometrical parameters. The developed microfluidic chip consisted of two channels (for perovskite precursor and isopropyl alcohol) with a valve or a porous membrane in-between. By adjusting the velocity rate in channels, CsPbBr3 particles with different geometries (cubes, NWs, rods, and others) were obtained. The obtained perovskite NWs supported lasing at room temperature in the visible range. Perovskite nanocrystals (NCs) can be also synthesized on a microfluidic platform. Lin et al. developed a microfluidic device that realized coaxial flow in a stable co-flow regime for the synthesis of all-inorganic perovskite nanocrystals[138] (Fig. 8A). The physical mixing of perovskite precursors and start of chemical reaction was initiated in the inner flow, whereas the outer fluid carried the growing NCs to the outlet. The whole growing procedure was monitored with an optical fiber absorption spectrometer and an optical fiber fluorescence spectrometer. Microfluidically obtained perovskite NCs were further applied for the fabrication of green and white LEDs. This group applied the same technology for the templated synthesis of perovskite NCs in order to improve their stability against temperature, light and water[139]. For this, microfluidically synthesized co-flowing perovskite NCs mixed with a hydrophobic photocurable precursor were divided into droplets by the continuous phase (sodium dodecyl sulfate). Afterwards, the obtained droplets were irradiated with UV light with their following solidification. The obtained green-emitting microspheres with encapsulated perovskite NCs showed stability in water up to 150 h.

In a series of works, a different approach for the microfluidic synthesis of lead halide perovskite NCs was developed by the group of deMello and Kovalenko. According to this synthesis approach, the segments are formed in a cross-mixing junction with further rapid heating to the desired temperature[24] (Fig. 8B). In order to obtain mixed perovskites CsPb(X/Y)3, two halides were mixed in advance in the T-junction mixer. Then, this mixture was delivered into the cross-mixer. The reaction time was controlled by the residence time of the segment in the heated zone. The perovskites obtained by means of the developed microfluidic system covered the whole visible spectral region with narrow emission linewidths. In another work, formation of formamidinium lead bromide and iodide (FAPbX3, X = Br, I) perovskite NCs was investigated using the developed microfluidic system based on segments[140]. Authors stressed the differences in the growth mechanisms of FAPbI3 and FAPbBr3: FAPBI3 formed cubic-shaped, NIR-emitting NCs, whereas FAPbBr3 resulted in platelet-like emission. Mixed FAPb(Cl1-xBrx)3 NCs with stable emission in the range of 440-520 nm were investigated by the same group in another work[141]. Authors monitored the influence of Cl/Br content, which was varied using a microfluidic system, on the morphology and optical performance of perovskites. Further, the same approach based on segmented microfluidic synthesis was applied for the synthesis of perovskite NCs with a more complex composition CsxFA1−xPb(Br1−yIy)3. The PL peak of the obtained NCs was tuned between 690 nm and 780 nm depending on the microfluidic synthesis conditions. Finally, these NCs were utilized for the fabrication of NIR LEDs with a quantum efficiency up to 5.9% at 735 nm[142]. Another group fabricated white-emitting LEDs using microfluidically obtained Cs4PbBr6 perovskite microcrystals. For the synthesis of perovskites, perovskite precursors (PbBr2 and CsBr) were mixed in the so-called T-mixer of a microfluidic system, and after that, the nucleation process and further growth of Cs4PbBr6 perovskite crystals were carried out under heating. The final incorporation of the obtained perovskite NCs with K2SiF6:Mn4+ red phosphor provided a wide color gamut of 119%[143]. It is worth noting that not only fluids can be applied as a carrier phase to realize segments in a microfluidic system, but also gases. Abdel-Latif et al. applied an inert gas (argon) for the formation of segments during the synthesis of lead halide NCs. Authors systematically studied the influence of ligand composition and halide salt source on the composition and optical performances of the obtained NCs using in situ optical spectroscopy[144].

Despite quite successful using of the developed conventional methods for obtaining perovskites, microfluidics synthesis approach makes the process much faster (several seconds/milliseconds) and reduces the synthesis temperature.

Droplet synthesis allows one to obtain homogeneous perovskites crystals of similar morphology (e.g., NWs) compared with the conventional one due to smaller concentration gradients in one reaction chamber (one drop), where it is possible to create the same conditions for synthesis in each drop.

Furthermore, synthesis of some perovskite materials requires the usage of harmful reagents, but microfluidics can automate the process of particles fabrication and allows conducting synthesis in sealed chips without air contact making the process environmentally friendly. However, some widely used materials for MFC chips (e.g., PDMS) can be not tolerable to a number of chemicals (isopropyl alcohol, dimethyl sulfoxide), which results in its swelling and destroying the chip geometry.

The application of perovskites in biomedical fields is limited due to their instability in water. However, in the recent work a way to overcome this obstacle was reported by encapsulating perovskites into nanomicelles consisting of polyethylene glycol-polycaprolactone block copolymers [145]. The results showed 24 h long stable photoluminescence of perovskite quantum dots in vivo. Another group demonstrated phospholipid encapsulation of CsPbBr3 perovskite and excellent biocompatibility (cell viability up to 94%) [146].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a new class of crystalline porous materials that has gained a lot of interest in the last few years. The unique structural property of MOFs is their extremely high porosity and, thus, increased internal surface area that can be utilized in such applications as data storage[147,148], gas adsorption[149,150], drug delivery[151–153], sensing[154] and others[15,155]. Optical properties of MOFs include optical absorption[156], PL, structural changes, and photocatalyst[157]. For instance, recently, optical switching of stimuli-responsive and photochromic-free HKUST-1 was demonstrated[15]. The main mechanism of this optical switching was explained by the dehydration-reversible structural shrinking of the MOF cage. Exposure to the NIR light led to a blue shift in the MOFs’ absorption band spectrum.

The conventional approach for MOFs synthesis is a solvothermal/hydrothermal synthesis. Beside that, there are such methods as microwave-assisted, ultrasound/sonochemical, electrochemical, mechanochemical, ionothermal, and dry gel conversion synthesis[158,159]. Solvothermal synthesis is based on electric heating in vials, tubes, or autoclave, and requires high temperatures in the range of 100-180 °C during a prolonged period of time, e.g., 12-96 h. Microwave-assisted synthesis of MOFs uses an alternating electric field to accelerate the synthesis by dipole rotation and ionic conduction[158,160]. Depending on the synthesis conditions, the duration of the reaction can vary from 10 to 60 min. In the ultrasound synthesis approach, the acceleration of the reaction time (around 30 min) is achieved by extreme local heating up to 5000 K[160]. MOFs synthesis techniques are comprehensively described in the works[159,161].

To achieve fast and monodisperse synthesis of MOFs with controllable porosity of the resulting structures, the microfluidic synthesis approach can also be applied. Faustini et al.[162] demonstrated synthesis of a number of MOFs such as HKUST-1, MOF-5, IRMOF-3, and UiO-66, and beside that, Co3BTC2@Ni3BTC2, MOF-5@diCH3-MOF-5 and Fe3O4@ZIF-8 core-shell MOF crystals, which were synthesized using droplet microfluidics with T-injector design. For HKUST-1, MOF-5, and UiO-66 synthesis, the precursor solutions were prepared by mixing metal salts and organic ligands into a homogeneous solution with solvents (N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol (EtOH) and deionized water (H2O)). To obtain drops, silicone oil was used as a continuous phase, while the whole system was heated up to 90°C for 1-12 min. By varying the reaction time, MOFs particles with different sizes were obtained.

Segmented flow microfluidic synthesis approach was applied for the fabrication of MIL-88B (Fe-MIL-88B-NH2, Fe-MIL-88B, and Fe-MIL-88B-Br) in the work[163]. In this case, the authors used a system of connected capillaries consisting of two intersections (Fig. 9A). The segments were formed at the 2nd intersection, while at the 1st the metal and ligand solutions were mixed. Cui et al.[164] synthesized monodisperse porous single-crystalline MOFs microcubes applying the droplet microfluidics with the T-junction capillary system. The various porosities of MOFs were achieved by adding polystyrene particles in the precursor solutions, which were composed of metal salt and organic ligand (Fig. 9B). The synthesis reaction was performed with different temperatures in the range of 45-90°C and took 36 h.

Conventional methods of MOFs synthesis demand high temperatures, considerable time and also some additional sources of energy (microwave-assisted, ultrasound) to accelerate the synthesis. Microfluidics enables all the benefits mentioned in other sections describing different types of optically responsive materials (reduced temperature, time and reagent consumption, as well as automation of synthesis route). Furthermore, the droplet microfluidic synthesis approach allows one to obtain the controllable porosity of MOFs since each drop acts as a small reaction chamber, where it is much easier to control important synthesis parameters and concentration of the reagents.

Due to the high biocompatibility and biodegradability MOF are well suited for bioapplication. Among that photonics-based applications can be mentioned: drug delivery, bioimaging and biosensing. The work [165] gives detailed review on this matter.

Polymeric NPs is another class of responsive nanomaterials that can be widely applied in various areas ranging from biomedicine to photonics[166–168]. Optically responsive polymeric NPs can either include photochromic moieties in their structure[169], or contain plasmonic[170]/high-refractive-index semiconducting[171]/highly crystalline magnetic[172] nanomaterials that are able to heat when exposed to light and, thus, induce structural changes in polymeric NPs. Conventional synthesis methods suppose preparation of polymeric NPs from a dispersion of preformed polymers or by polymerization of monomers. One should distinguish the following approaches for the polymeric NPs synthesis: solvent evaporation[173], emulsification-solvent diffusion[174], solvent displacement[175], and monomer polymerization methods[176]. The duration of these approaches can vary from 2 to 24 h.

Microfluidics technologies were also applied to improve shortcomings of the conventional synthesis of polymeric NPs such as lack of control over particle size, morphology and composition. Baby et al. applied flow focusing regime for the synthesis of uniform poly(lactide-co-glycolide)-b-polyethylene glycol (PLGA-PEG)NPs with sizes of 50–150 nm. Variations both in geometrical sizes of the MFC channel and in the flow rate ratios resulted in a narrow particle size distribution. Authors achieved an increased production rate of (PLGA-PEG) NPs of 288 mg/h using the developed microfluidic system[177]. Droplet-based approach was developed by the group of Köhler to produce poly(tripropylene glycol diacrylate) (poly-TPGDA) microparticles with various sizes (40-500 μm). Generation of droplets with monomers was realized at the T-junction by the shear force of the continuous aqueous phase. Apart from monomers, the dispersed phase also contained organic dyes, cross-linker and photo-initiator, which induced polymerization in response to UV light irradiation. The combination of particles with different sizes and colors with different concentrations of mixed fluorescence dyes during the synthesis resulted in a huge amount of patterns that can be applied in biological screening[178]. A similar microfluidic setup was used for the synthesis of polymer particles with embedded Ag NPs in a wide size range (30-600 μm) for the SERS sensing applications. Authors demonstrated that increasing Ag NPs content in the polymer particles led to the enhancement of Raman signal from the analytes (amino acids and vitamins), which is favorable for the creation of a particle-based SERS sensor for the identification of various biomolecules[179]. Lanthanide nanophosphors can also be used to functionalize polymeric NPs for the synthesis of spectrally encoded fluorescence beads for multiplexing. Gerver et al. reported on droplet-based microfluidic synthesis of hydrophilic PEG–acrylate polymer particles doped with lanthanide nanophosphors. Synthesis of polymer particles was achieved by preparing a mixture of nanophosphors in advance, their further suspension in monomer and by following photopolymerization of defined droplets. The resulting particles were monodispersed with a unique spectral code[180] (Fig. 10A). Gas-liquid microfluidic reactor was developed in order to synthesize photoresponsive polymeric NPs composed of block copolymer poly(o-nitrobenzyl acrylate)-b-polydimethylacrylamide (PNBA-b-PDMA). In this study, Ar gas was applied to shear the continuous aqueous phase containing PNBA-b-PDMA. Geometry (spheres, short cylinders, large compound micelles and unique spooled cylinders) of the obtained NPs depended on the applied flow rates. The fastest flow led to the formation of spooled cylinders, which dissociated upon light irradiation[181] (Fig. 10B). Lone et al. demonstrated synthesis based on droplet microfluidics of fluorescent polymer Janus NPs, using UV light-directed phase separation of the homogenous photo-polymerizable water-in-oil emulsion[182]. In the work [183] a multilamination micromixer is used to fabricate continuously nanoparticles of methacrylic polymers. An adjustment of the synthesis parameters gave a range of the resulting polymer particles from 76 to 217 nm. Besides that, authors demonstrate that micromixing leads to the formation of smaller particles [184]. The same group has demonstrated synthesis of poly(acrylamide) core-poly(tri(propylene glycol) diacrylate) shell particles with controlled core diameter and shell thickness by adjusting operating parameters [185]. By tuning the fluids’ flow rates and continuous phase viscosity it is shown that particles size can be precisely controlled. It was obtained in a two co-axial capillaries microfluidic device and the empirical laws for predicting core-shell and rod-like polymer particles size are given. All the studies discussed in this section demonstrate the ability of microfluidic platforms to generate optically responsive polymer particles with a narrow size distribution and well-controlled optical properties.

The main advantage of the microfluidics approach used for polymeric NPs synthesis is the micro-size reactor (drop). Polymerization of monomers by conventional methods is a long process due to the surface to volume ratio. In each drop at microscale all these effects occur homogeneously. Also, faster heat and mass transfer enables enhanced mixing.

Polymer particles have numerous applications in biophotonics. Among them the drug delivery platforms can be highlighted. There are several mechanisms of polymer chain disruption due to the external light exposure that are listed in the Ref. [85].

To date, conventional synthesis methods of optically responsive nanostructured materials, such as plasmonic, photoluminescent and shape-changeable NPs, are more widespread in comparison with microfluidic ones. However, microfluidic approaches currently are an actively developing area for the synthesis of such nanomaterials, since the optical properties of NPs are strongly defined by their synthesis conditions. Besides, fabrication of NPs that requires special synthesis conditions (inert atmosphere, temperature, etc.) is easier to realize in an MFC. Due to the possibility to vary and to control the different stages of NPs formation (nucleation, growth), the resulting nanomaterials can be fabricated with the desired size distribution and polydispersity index.
The microfluidic synthesis allows reducing the time of NPs fabrication, using milder temperature conditions, and strict control over the mixing of reagents at the defined ratio (Table 2 ).

Automated large-scale production and reproducibility of synthesis products enables adoption of microfluidic technology as a basic tool for the synthesis in modern chemical laboratories. This technology simplifies the synthetic route of multistep nanomaterials’ fabrication, allowing researchers without a strong chemical background to perform synthesis of the required nanomaterials. Moreover, microfluidic technologies are capable of creating one-step synthesis of multicomponent NPs (e.g., functional core-shell particles), which are nowadays under intensive investigation. However, analytical equipment integrated in an MFC and sophisticated real-time control of the reaction outcomes are yet to be developed for microfluidic synthesis. Therefore, the forthcoming research in microfluidics synthesis of optically responsive NPs will focus on the fine tuning of their configurations and conformations with real-time monitoring of the synthesis product in order to achieve unique optical properties for the further optical devices.

As highlighted above, synthesis of nanomaterials by the conventional approaches implies time-consuming processes and often results in a broad size distribution. In turn, microfluidic synthesis methods reduce the reaction times (as indicated in Table 2) due to a higher rate of heat transfer and precise regulation of synthesis conditions such as full control over reagent concentrations, efficient mixing and flow adjustment.

There are several well-known conventional methods used to synthesize plasmonic NPs/semiconductor QDs/perovskite NPs/polymeric NPs that offer a fast room-temperature approach, but at the same time, they require sophisticated equipment and high consumption of reagents. Microfluidic synthesis approaches of plasmonic NPs/semiconductor QDs are able to solve this limitation since all the processes can be implemented in one chip. As for the perovskites’ synthesis, microfluidics facilitates the process by automatization, and enables the synthesis to be conducted in the air (sealed MFC prevents air from entering the reaction channels). Microfluidic approach for the fabrication of polymeric NPs accelerates the synthesis procedure and enables a precise control over particle sizes. All the above mentioned benefits of microfluidic synthesis approach (small reagent consumption, rapid heat and mass transfer, and most importantly, highly efficient mixing, which lead to reduction of the synthesis time and temperature) are also valid for the fabrication of CDs/MOFs with the narrowed size distributions.

Despite all the mentioned advantages of the microfluidic approach of NPs synthesis, it also has several restrictions. In some cases, microfluidic synthesis implies a reaction of pre-prepared reagents. In other words, all the complex reagents, e.g., for MOFs/perovskites synthesis, should be prepared before they are loaded into MFC. Another limitation that can be considered as a drawback is that the major MFC fabrication technique is “soft” lithography employing liquid polymers (e.g., PDMS), which are not always tolerable to some chemicals that are required for the NPs synthesis. For example, dimethylformamide or ethanol, which are used as common solvents in MOFs fabrication, cause the swelling of the PDMS channels in an MFC, distorting the initial channels’ geometry[186]. To overcome this, other, more stable materials (glass, silicon, thermoplastics, etc.) can be utilized for MFCs fabricating; however, this requires a more complex MFC fabrication process in the cases of glass or Si wafer (glass etching, Si wafer etching). In the case of polymers, thermal pressing (molding) or other methods of MFC fabrication can be used, which are comparable in cost and complexity with “soft” lithography). Finally, it should be considered that the final yield of the reaction product using microfluidic approach can be lower compared to the conventional methods due to the smaller operating volumes for reagents.
It is also worth mentioning that all the particles mentioned in this review has shown promising results in nano- and biophotonics applications (Table 3 ).
